title: All that boilerplate... gone
instruction:
Every class you have written needed:
- `__init__` to store the fields
- `__repr__` to make it printable
- `__eq__` to make == work properly

For a class with 5 fields, that is 20+ lines of setup code. Every. Single. Time.

`@dataclass` generates all of it automatically from your field declarations.

Run this. Compare: same result, half the code.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

# Without @dataclass — 12 lines of boilerplate:
class ProductManual:
    def __init__(self, name, price, in_stock):
        self.name = name
        self.price = price
        self.in_stock = in_stock
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Product(name={self.name!r}, price={self.price!r}, in_stock={self.in_stock!r})"

# With @dataclass — 4 lines:
@dataclass
class Product:
    name: str
    price: float
    in_stock: bool

p = Product("Laptop", 999.99, True)
print(p)
print(p.name)
successMessage: @dataclass generated __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ automatically. Same result, dramatically less code. This is Python 3.7+ and it's one of the best additions to the language.

title: Create a dataclass
instruction:
Fields are declared with type annotations:

```python
@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float
```

The type annotation (`: float`) tells @dataclass what the field is called and what type to show in repr. Python doesn't enforce the type at runtime — but it makes your code clear and tools like mypy can check it.

Create a `User` dataclass with `name`, `email`, and `age` fields. Create two instances.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

# TODO: define User dataclass with name, email, age
# Create two instances and print them

codeChecks.message: Create at least one User instance.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three fields, no __init__ written, no __repr__ written. @dataclass handled everything. Look at the print output — it shows User(name='...', email='...', age=...) automatically.

title: Default values
instruction:
Fields can have defaults — used when the argument is not passed:

```python
@dataclass
class Config:
    host: str = "localhost"
    port: int = 8080
    debug: bool = False
```

One rule: fields WITH defaults must come AFTER fields WITHOUT.

Add `is_active` (defaults to `True`) and `role` (defaults to `"user"`).
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class User:
    name: str
    email: str
    # TODO: add is_active with default True and role with default "user"

user1 = User("Alice", "alice@example.com")
user2 = User("Bob", "bob@example.com", is_active=False, role="admin")
print(user1)
print(user2)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: user1 uses all defaults. user2 overrides two fields. Defaults in dataclasses work exactly like default parameters in functions — optional after required.

title: __eq__ is automatic
instruction:
One of @dataclass's best tricks: it generates `__eq__` for you. Two instances with identical field values are equal.

Run this to confirm.
starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float

p1 = Point(3.0, 4.0)
p2 = Point(3.0, 4.0)
p3 = Point(1.0, 2.0)

print(p1 == p2)  # True — same values
print(p1 == p3)  # False — different values
print(p1 is p2)  # False — different objects in memory
successMessage: p1 and p2 are equal (same values) but not identical (different objects). @dataclass gave you value-based equality for free — something you had to write manually in Chapter 16.

title: frozen=True — immutable dataclass
instruction:
`@dataclass(frozen=True)` makes the class immutable — like a namedtuple, but with the readability of @dataclass.

Perfect for configuration, coordinate pairs, colours — anything that should never change after creation.

Run this.
successMessage: FrozenInstanceError — you cannot modify a frozen dataclass. Frozen dataclasses can also be used as dictionary keys (regular ones can't). Perfect for immutable value objects.

title: __post_init__ — validate after init
instruction:
`__post_init__` runs automatically right after `__init__` — perfect for validation:

```python
def __post_init__(self):
    if self.age < 0:
        raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative")
```

Add `__post_init__` to validate that price is positive and stock is non-negative.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: __post_init__ is the sweet spot — @dataclass handles the boilerplate, you add the custom validation. Best of both worlds.

title: Build an order system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build two dataclasses:
- `Item(name, price, quantity)` — **frozen** (items don't change once ordered). Add a `total` property using `__post_init__` or `field(default_factory=...)`
- `Order(items, customer_name)` — mutable, with a `total()` method that sums item totals

Create an order with 3 items and print the total.

codeChecks.message: Make Item frozen — order items should not change.
Add a total() method to Order.

successMessage: Immutable items inside a mutable order — clean design. @dataclass handling all the boilerplate, you writing only the important logic. Chapter 17 done!
